title: West African Suya-Style Grilled Chicken

ingredients.name: chicken breast or thigh, sliced into thin 1 cm strips
visual_checks: Thighs stay juicier on the grill; breast strips cook faster
ingredients.notes: Thighs stay juicier on the grill; breast strips cook faster

subs.name: Paneer slices (250 g) for a vegetarian suya; grill for 2 minutes per side only so the paneer does not harden

ingredients.name: roasted peanuts, very finely ground (the yaji spice base)
visual_checks: Ground roasted peanuts form the dry coating that is the essence of suya
ingredients.notes: Ground roasted peanuts form the dry coating that is the essence of suya

subs.name: Ground roasted sunflower seeds for a peanut-free version; the texture is similar but flavour is milder
Kashmiri chilli powder for vivid colour with moderate heat; smoked paprika for smokiness without the heat

ingredients.name: dry ginger powder (soonth)
visual_checks: Dry ginger powder (soonth) is traditional; fresh ginger paste (double quantity) can substitute
ingredients.notes: Dry ginger powder (soonth) is traditional; fresh ginger paste (double quantity) can substitute

subs.name: Fresh ginger paste (double the quantity) rubbed into the spice mix works well if dry ginger powder (soonth) is unavailable

steps.text: Combine the finely ground roasted peanuts, red chilli powder, ginger powder, garlic, coriander powder, cumin, cinnamon, black pepper and salt in a bowl — this is the yaji (suya spice blend). Add oil and mix into a thick, slightly sticky paste that will cling to the chicken.

Add the thin-sliced chicken strips to the bowl and rub the yaji coating thoroughly onto every surface of the meat. Marinate for at least 15 minutes at room temperature, or up to 2 hours in the fridge for deeper flavour. Thread onto skewers if desired, or keep flat for tawa grilling.

Heat a tawa, grill pan or heavy frying pan over high heat until very hot. Brush lightly with a drop of oil. Place the marinated chicken strips in a single layer — do not crowd the pan — and cook for 3–4 minutes per side until the coating is darkly charred at the edges and the chicken is fully cooked through with no pink remaining.

Rest the grilled chicken for 2 minutes. The peanut coating should have formed a dark, fragrant crust on the surface. Serve immediately on a bed of sliced raw onion with extra yaji sprinkled over and a squeeze of lime on the side. Traditionally accompanied with sliced white bread or plain rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: peanut → Ground roasted sunflower seeds for a peanut-free version; the texture is similar but flavour is milder; chicken → Paneer slices (250 g) for a vegetarian suya; grill for 2 minutes per side only so the paneer does not harden; ginger → Fresh ginger paste (double the quantity) rubbed into the spice mix works well if dry ginger powder (soonth) is unavailable; red-chilli-powder → Kashmiri chilli powder for vivid colour with moderate heat; smoked paprika for smokiness without the heat
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Ground moongphali (peanuts) are the suya rub's key ingredient — available roasted at Indian stores; use unsalted where possible or reduce added salt. Dry ginger powder (soonth) from the Indian pantry is exactly what the recipe calls for. Cook on a cast-iron tawa over a high flame for the best char. The spice profile is close to a dry tandoor rub but with ground peanuts as the binder instead of yogurt.
